narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income level, and planned full-time enrollment status. The model flagged your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 as the single biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, since it places you well below the typical top performers among those who passed the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as a strong counterbalance, pulling the prediction toward success because it's close to the average for bar passers, even if slightly under at 3.10 compared to their 3.24. Your LSAT score of 37 also helped in the same way, nearly matching the 37.27 average of successful students and softening the overall risk assessment. While your high family income bracket aligns with a smaller but successful subset of passers, and full-time status is the norm for them, these didn't carry as much weight in the decision. Compared to admitted students, who typically rank much higher in later-year deciles like 5.91 on average for Year 3, your profile showed a concerning dip in performance trajectory that outweighed your solid entrance credentials. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just how these key academic markers stacked up against patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above the passer average or focusing on strategies to improve your upper-year rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or course selection. You might also gather letters from professors highlighting your resilience and growth potential to supplement future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—reapplying after strengthening those areas could shift the odds in your favor. Keep pushing; your full-time commitment and strong starting GPA show you have the foundation to build on.
